topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. The subject line was innocuous enough—a notification about a published piece—but the content made my stomach clench. I clicked it open, and there it was: my article, complete with my analysis and research, credited entirely to a completely different author. A cold wave washed over me, starting at my fingertips and creeping up my neck. I stared at the screen, my breath catching in my throat. Every carefully constructed confidence I’d built over the last year seemed to evaporate in that single moment. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, running a hand through my already disheveled hair. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my chest. I felt a profound sense of exposure, as if every secret I held was now laid bare for judgment. I deleted the email, but the residue of that revelation clung to me, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

When I finally saw the publication details, the world tilted. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure of my contribution. My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to steady them. I reread the attribution three times, each repetition digging a deeper furrow of distress into my brow. A flush of heat rose instantly to my cheeks, and I quickly averted my gaze, focusing instead on a dust mote dancing in a sunbeam slicing through the window. The air felt thick, hard to draw in. I walked to the mirror, my reflection looking strained and hollow-eyed. There was a deep, bitter taste in my mouth. I wanted to scream, to demand an explanation, but only a dry, shaky exhale escaped my lips. The realization settled in my gut—a heavy, sinking feeling that made every movement feel clumsy and wrong.

[story 3]

The notification popped up, stark white against the dark background of my monitor. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm that drowned out the ambient noise of the office. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and forced myself to read the text again. The words were mine. My unique perspective, the hours I’d poured into that manuscript—all belonging to someone else now. A sudden, intense prickling sensation ran down my spine, making me instinctively pull my shoulders inward, hunching slightly as if trying to physically shrink away from the light. I picked up my coffee mug, but my fingers were too stiff to hold it properly. I took a long, slow sip, the bitter liquid doing nothing to soothe the churning turmoil inside. I felt utterly exposed, stripped bare of the authorship I had fought so hard to claim.
